perturbation.original_text: 5.3 The Distributor must pay the full amount invoiced to it by the Supplier in USD$'s within thirty (45) days of the date of invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 The Distributor must pay the full amount invoiced to it by the Supplier in USD$'s within ninety (90) days of the date of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 90 days might violate prompt payment laws in some states, especially if the contract involves construction or other industries with specific payment deadlines.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORC § 4113.61 addresses prompt payment for construction contracts in Ohio. While this agreement isn't explicitly a construction contract, extending the payment period to 90 days could potentially be challenged if the agreement involves aspects related to construction or improvements, especially if it causes undue hardship or violates industry standards for prompt payment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 4113.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 4113.61 | Time limitations for payments to subcontractors and materialmen. Ohio Revised Code / Title 41 Labor and Industry / Chapter 4113 Miscellaneous Labor Provisions Effective: September 29, 2011 Latest Legislation: House Bill 153 - 129th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A)(1) If a subcontractor or material supplier submits an application or request for payment or an invoice for materials to a contractor in sufficient time to allow the contractor to include the application, request, or invoice in the contractor's own pay request submitted to an owner, the contractor, within ten calendar days after receipt of payment from the owner for improvements to property, shall pay to the: (a) Subcontractor, an amount that is equal to the percentage of completion of the subcontractor's contract allowed by the owner for the amount of labor or work performed; (b) Material supplier, an amount that is equal to all or that portion of the invoice for materials which represents the materials furnished by the material supplier. The contractor may reduce the amount paid by any retainage provision contained in the contract, invoice, or purchase order between the contractor and the subcontractor or material supplier, and may withhold amounts that may be necessary to resolve disputed liens or claims involving the work or labor performed or material furnished by the subcontractor or material supplier. If the contractor fails to comply with division (A)(1) of this section, the contractor shall pay the subcontractor or material supplier, in addition to the payment due, interest in the amount of eighteen per cent per annum of the payment due, beginning on the
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment period to 90 days, potentially violating prompt payment laws. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this could be problematic, especially in industries like construction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, ORC § 4113.61, specifies payment timelines for contractors to subcontractors/material suppliers, requiring payment within ten days after the contractor receives payment from the owner. Although this snippet directly addresses construction and subcontractors, the principle of prompt payment is established, and extending payment terms to 90 days could be seen as contradicting this principle, creating an inconsistency regarding payment obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 10.2 The Distributor shall, within five days of a matter which may result in a Relevant Claim: (a) give the Supplier written notice of the details of the matter
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2 The Distributor shall, within one day of a matter which may result in a Relevant Claim: (a) give the Supplier written notice of the details of the matter
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification timeframe to one day for potential liability claims could be deemed unreasonably short and conflict with the 'reasonable time' standard often applied in insurance and contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORC § 3937.18 relates to uninsured or underinsured motorist coverage and requires prompt notification of claims. While this isn't directly product liability, it reflects a general principle that notification periods should be reasonable. A one-day notification for a product liability claim might be seen as insufficient and thus against public policy as reflected in the statute's emphasis on "prompt" notification and the insured's cooperation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 3937.18 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 3937.18 | Uninsured and underinsured motorist coverage. Ohio Revised Code / Title 39 Insurance / Chapter 3937 Casualty Insurance; Motor Vehicle Insurance Effective: March 14, 2023 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 56 - 134th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Any policy of insurance delivered or issued for delivery in this state with respect to any motor vehicle registered or principally garaged in this state that insures against loss resulting from liability imposed by law for bodily injury or death suffered by any person arising out of the ownership, maintenance, or use of a motor vehicle, may, but is not required to, include uninsured motorist coverage, underinsured motorist coverage, or both uninsured and underinsured motorist coverages. Unless otherwise defined in the policy or any endorsement to the policy, "motor vehicle," for purposes of the uninsured motorist coverage, underinsured motorist coverage, or both uninsured and underinsured motorist coverages, means a self-propelled vehicle designed for use and principally used on public roads, including an automobile, truck, semi-tractor, motorcycle, and bus. "Motor vehicle" also includes a motor home, provided the motor home is not stationary and is not being used as a temporary or permanent residence or office. "Motor vehicle" does not include a trolley, streetcar, trailer, railroad engine, railroad car, motorized bicycle, golf cart, off-road recreational vehicle, snowmobile, fork lift, aircraft, watercraft, construction equipment, farm tractor or other vehicle designed and principally used for agricultural purposes, mobile home, vehicle traveling on treads or rails, or any simil
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification timeframe from five days to one day, which could be deemed unreasonably short and conflict with the 'reasonable time' standard. The LLM explanation relates this to ORC § 3937.18 which requires prompt notification of claims in the context of uninsured motorist coverage, reflecting a general principle that notification periods should be reasonable. While Snippet 1 does not directly address product liability or specify a timeframe, it establishes that promptness and cooperation are expected. The changed text, by imposing a one-day notification period, increases the risk that a distributor would be unable to comply with the standard, leading to potential non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 11.1 This agreement begins on the Commencement Date and, subject to clause 11.2, shall continue for an initial term of one (1) years (Initial Term) and indefinitely after that until terminated by either party giving at least twelve (12) months' prior written notice to expire on or after the expiry date of the initial term.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.1 This agreement begins on the Commencement Date and, subject to clause 11.2, shall continue for an initial term of one (1) years (Initial Term) and indefinitely after that until terminated by either party giving at least one (1) months' prior written notice to expire on or after the expiry date of the initial term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to one month may violate state franchise laws if the distributorship meets the criteria for a franchise agreement, which often require longer notice periods for termination to protect the distributor's investment.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORC § 1331.62, part of Ohio's franchise laws, requires good cause and written notice (at least 30 days, but potentially longer depending on circumstances) for the termination of a franchise. If this distributorship agreement is considered a franchise under Ohio law, reducing the notice period to one month would violate this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top Number Not Found No Ohio Revised Code section number corresponds to "1331.62".
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from twelve months to one month. The Law Explanation indicates that ORC § 1331.62, part of Ohio's franchise laws, requires at least 30 days' notice and potentially longer for franchise terminations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'No Ohio Revised Code section number corresponds to '1331.62'.' However, the Perturbation Explanation describes that reducing the termination notice period to one month may violate state franchise laws if the distributorship meets the criteria for a franchise agreement. Thus, the reduction to one month creates a potential contradiction with franchise laws, as it provides significantly less notice than potentially required, creating an Inconsistency Legal Contradiction. The changed text sets a requirement that violates state law.
